Elihu rebuked Job for justifying himself more than God, and directed his attention to the heavens. They are far above us, and God is far above them; how much then is he beyond our reach, either regarding our sins or accepting our service! We have no reason to complain if we do not receive what we expect, but should be thankful that we have something better than we deserve.

Job complained that God did not regard the cries of the oppressed against their oppressors. He did not know how to reconcile this with God’s justice and government. Elihu resolves the difficulty. People do not notice the mercies they enjoy during and under their afflictions, nor are thankful for them; therefore, they cannot expect that God should deliver them from affliction. He gives songs in the night; when our condition is dark and sad, there is something in God’s providence and promise which is sufficient to support us, and to enable us even to rejoice in tribulation. When we only focus on our afflictions, and neglect the consolations of God that are treasured up for us, it is just in God to reject our prayers. Even things that can harm the body cannot hurt the soul. If we ask God to remove an affliction, and it is not removed, the reason is not because the Lord’s power has failed or his ear is closed; but because we are not sufficiently humble.

As when we prosper, we are ready to think our success will never end; so when we suffer, we are ready to think our hardship will never cease. But to conclude that tomorrow must be like today is as foolish as to think the weather, whether fair or foul, will always remain so. When Job looked up to God, he had no reason to speak despairingly. There is a day of judgment, when all that seems wrong will be found right, and all that seems dark and difficult will be made clear. And if there is Divine wrath in our troubles, it is because we argue with God, are fretful, and distrust Divine Providence. This was Job’s case. Elihu was directed by God to humble Job, for he had both spoken without purpose and multiplied words without knowledge. Let us be reminded, in our afflictions, not so much to emphasize the extent of our suffering, as the greatness of God's mercy.
